metadata.working_title: Peafowl: An Alignment-free Method for Phylogeny Estimation using Maximum Likelihood

abstract_sketch.problem: Inferring phylogenies from whole-genome data is increasingly needed for genome skims and species whose genomes contain extensive rearrangements, but alignment remains a computational bottleneck and is brittle under rearrangement.
abstract_sketch.gap: Alignment-free methods avoid multiple sequence alignment but the vast majority are distance-based and rely on simplified pairwise distance models; no widely used maximum-likelihood alignment-free tool exists, despite maximum-likelihood methods being more accurate than distance-based ones in the alignment-based setting.
abstract_sketch.approach: We encode presence and absence of k-mers of a data-driven length in a binary matrix per species and estimate the maximum-likelihood tree topology under the two-state BINGAMMA substitution model in RAxML. The k-mer length k_entropy is selected by maximizing cumulative entropy over 5000 sampled rows across odd k from 9 to 31.
abstract_sketch.key_results: On seven real datasets (7 Primates, 14 Drosophila genome skims, 29 E.coli/Shigella, 25 Labroidei fish, 14 plants, 27 E.coli/Shigella, 8 Yersinia) Peafowl achieves the lowest normalized Robinson-Foulds distance on three datasets, ties the best on fish (nRF = 0.05) and primates (nRF = 0), and reconstructs the Yersinia reference tree exactly (nRF = 0) in non-canonical counting mode, on which most tools achieve nRF above 0.8.
abstract_sketch.impact: A likelihood-based alignment-free framework closes a long-standing methodological gap and mirrors the historical improvement of likelihood over distance in alignment-based phylogenetics, suggesting a clear path to refined alignment-free phylogenomics.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Phylogenetic tree inference from genome data is a central problem in bioinformatics and systematics.
Two traditional paradigms: distance-based (e.g., UPGMA, neighbor-joining) and character-based (maximum parsimony, maximum likelihood).
Within alignment-based inference, maximum likelihood has repeatedly been shown more accurate and robust than distance-based approaches, especially when model assumptions are violated.

Alignment-based methods require multiple sequence alignment (MSA), which is memory- and time-intensive, NP-hard to optimize exactly, and brittle to rearrangement events (translocation, inversion) common in whole-genome data.

Alignment-free methods avoid MSA and scale to whole genomes and to genome skims where assembly is impractical, but accuracy lags alignment-based approaches; almost all alignment-free tools are distance-based.
Prior work by Hoehl and Ragan used a Bayesian k-mer-based method, but no mature maximum-likelihood alignment-free tool exists.

Contribution: Peafowl builds a k-mer presence/absence binary matrix and runs RAxML under the BINGAMMA binary-trait model to get a maximum-likelihood tree, with an entropy-based procedure for k selection.
We evaluate on seven real datasets, including five from the AFproject benchmark, and compare against nine state-of-the-art alignment-free tools.

subsections.title: Overview
content_bullets: Four-stage pipeline: k-mer generation; binary matrix construction; k-mer length selection by cumulative entropy; maximum-likelihood tree estimation with RAxML under BINGAMMA.
Figure 1 summarizes the pipeline.

subsections.title: Generating k-mers
content_bullets: Use Jellyfish 2.2.4 to enumerate k-mers for odd k in {9, 11, ..., 31}.
Two modes: canonical (k-mer and reverse complement collapsed) and non-canonical (treated as distinct); canonical is default.
Figure 2 illustrates how the existence of k-mers depends on length.

subsections.title: Binary matrix construction
content_bullets: Matrix X with rows indexed by unique k-mers and columns by species; X_ij = 1 if k-mer i (or its reverse complement in canonical mode) occurs in species j, else 0.
Hash table maps each k-mer to the species carrying it; matrix entries are filled by iterating hash indices.

subsections.title: Entropy-based k-mer length selection
content_bullets: Per-row binary entropy H(X_i) = - sum_{x in {0,1}} f(x) log f(x) over m species.
Cumulative entropy C_entropy(k) = sum over p = 5000 randomly sampled rows of H(X_i).
Choose k_entropy = argmax_k C_entropy(k) over the scanned odd values.
Rationale: intermediate-frequency k-mers (neither ubiquitous nor extremely rare) carry the most phylogenetic information.

subsections.title: Tree estimation
content_bullets: Feed binary matrix for k_entropy to RAxML 8.2.4 under BINGAMMA (two-state model with a gamma-distributed site-rate prior).
Report tree topology only; branch-length estimation is deferred because neighboring k-mers are not independent under a single substitution.

subsections.title: Implementation
content_bullets: Peafowl is implemented in C++ and shell scripts and is available at https://github.com/hasin-abrar/Peafowlrepo.
Jellyfish is chosen for k-mer counting based on the comparison by Zhang et al.

subsections.title: Datasets and benchmarking
content_bullets: Seven real datasets: 7 Primates mitochondrial; 14 Drosophila genome skims (100 Mb); 29 E.coli/Shigella assembled; 25 Labroidei fish mitochondrial; 14 plants; 27 E.coli/Shigella full; 8 Yersinia full.
Compared methods: FFP, co-phylog, mash, Skmer, FSWM/Read-SpaM, andi, phylonium, Multi-SpaM, CAFE-cvtree.
Metric: normalized Robinson-Foulds (nRF) between estimated and reference tree, computed with PHYLIP; distance-only methods post-processed with neighbor-joining and UPGMA in MEGA-X.

subsections.title: Selection of k-mer lengths
content_bullets: For all datasets, the k with the highest cumulative entropy corresponds to the lowest observed nRF across scanned odd k.
On 7 Primates, both maximum entropy and nRF = 0 occur at k = 9; a secondary dip at k = 23 is attributed to the small number of species.

subsections.title: Primates and Drosophila
content_bullets: Peafowl reconstructs the 7 Primates reference tree exactly (nRF = 0); so do andi, Multi-SpaM, and FFP.
Mash, Skmer, and co-phylog misplace Gibbon as sister to hominines, failing the (orangutan, (gorilla, (chimpanzee, human))) relationship.
On Drosophila, Peafowl, Skmer, and phylonium achieve the lowest nRF; Peafowl and Skmer produce identical trees that differ from the reference by one branch in the mauritiana/simulans/sechellia clade.

subsections.title: AFproject genome-based phylogeny
content_bullets: Peafowl nRF: 0.23 on 29 E.coli/Shigella; 0.05 on 25 Labroidei fish; 0.36 on 14 plants.
On the 25 Labroidei fish dataset, Peafowl ties mash and FSWM for the lowest nRF.
On 29 E.coli/Shigella, phylonium is best; on 14 plants, co-phylog, mash, and Multi-SpaM are best.
For the plant dataset, k is scanned only over {9, ..., 17} to avoid resource exhaustion; k = 9 gives degenerate all-1 rows.

subsections.title: Horizontal gene transfer datasets (E.coli full and Yersinia)
content_bullets: On 27 E.coli/Shigella full genomes, Peafowl nRF = 0.17; best are co-phylog, andi, phylonium at nRF = 0.08.
On 8 Yersinia with canonical counting, Peafowl nRF = 1; most tools have nRF > 0.8, only two are below 0.8; CAFE-cvtree is best.
With non-canonical counting, Peafowl reconstructs the Yersinia reference tree exactly (nRF = 0), because inversions become visible when a k-mer and its reverse complement are distinguished.
Non-canonical counting yields substantially higher entropy than canonical on Yersinia, consistent with more informative binary columns.

subsections.title: Runtime and memory
content_bullets: Measurements taken on Intel Core i7-7700 at 3.60 GHz with 8 processors and 32 GB RAM.
Per-dataset runtime, unzipped size, and peak memory are reported in Table 1.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Peafowl demonstrates that a likelihood-based alignment-free approach is feasible and competitive with the state of the art.
Performance varies substantially across datasets; no single method dominates, echoing prior AFproject conclusions.
Non-canonical counting unlocks inversion signal, explaining the dramatic Yersinia result.

Limitations: small taxa cap (30); distant species yield few shared k-mers; missing regions in sequences degrade performance; counts are discarded; branch lengths are not estimated; unassembled reads are not yet supported.
Future directions: richer count-based models; extension to reads; branch-length estimation; scaling to more taxa; mixture-model priors on site rates across k-mer classes.

sections.title: Conclusions
content_bullets: Peafowl introduces the first maximum-likelihood alignment-free phylogeny tool.
It achieves the lowest nRF on three of seven datasets and matches the reference tree on Yersinia under non-canonical counting.

The gap between alignment-free and alignment-based methods may close as likelihood-based alignment-free methods mature, mirroring the historical improvement of likelihood over distance in alignment-based phylogenetics.
